seo.page_title: 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV import to NZ
seo.meta_description: Import a 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV from Japan to NZ. Lower running costs, plug-in hybrid flexibility and clear landed pricing, including compliance steps.
seo.h1_tag: Import a 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV to NZ
seo.og_title: 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV | Import to NZ
seo.og_description: Looking at a 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV? Import from Japan for plug-in hybrid efficiency, AWD practicality and clear landed costs across NZ.
secondary_keywords: import plug in hybrid SUV NZ

blocks.hero_h1: Import a 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV to NZ
blocks.hero_subtitle: Plug-in hybrid SUV comfort with running costs that suit NZ commuting and family trips, without paying local dealer premiums.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: Around town it runs mostly on electric, then on the open road the 2.0L petrol engine and twin motors settle into an efficient cruise. The 12 kWh battery and EV-only range up to 35 km cover most school runs and city commutes, while AWD and five-seat SUV practicality handle wet roads, gravel and family gear.

savings_block.headline: What you can save vs buying a 2018 Outlander PHEV in NZ
savings_block.body: Most 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEVs we source land between about $22,000 and $35,000 depending on grade, mileage and features. That typically undercuts similar NZ dealer stock once you factor in their margin. We show you a full landed estimate up front, including shipping and NZ entry certification, so you can compare it cleanly to local listings.

cta_form.headline: Find your Outlander PHEV — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: Walk through a 2018 Outlander PHEV showing the EV drive modes, AWD controls and how the charging port and cables are laid out.
blocks.main_usp_deep: The 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV combines a 2.0L petrol engine with twin electric motors and a 12 kWh battery, so 4–5L/100km in real-world NZ driving is typical for these. Around town you can lean on EV mode for short trips, then let the hybrid system manage longer runs without range anxiety. AWD and multiple drive modes give extra confidence on wet or loose surfaces, while regenerative braking helps in stop–start traffic and on hills. From Japan you also see higher spec examples, which usually means more comfort and safety kit for the money.

buyer_persona.headline: Is this car right for you?
buyer_persona.body: You’re likely a tech-comfort focused buyer who wants lower running costs but still needs a practical family SUV. You don’t mind plugging in at home if it means fewer visits to the servo and a quieter commute. If you like the idea of EV driving without worrying about long-trip range, the Outlander PHEV is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: We’re an NZ-based team that’s been importing from Japan since 2009, with more than 1,000 vehicles sourced for local buyers. You get direct access to Japanese auctions, full auction sheets and photos, and clear explanations of what each grade and comment means. We map out the landed cost before you commit, including shipping and NZ entry certification steps. Our role is to help you weigh up the numbers and the condition so you can decide if a given Outlander PHEV looks reasonably well bought.

car_specs_benefits.spec: 2.0L inline-4 petrol engine paired with twin electric motors (front and rear)
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Gives smooth, quiet EV running in town with enough combined power for open-road passing and hills.
Supports regular EV-only use for short commutes, school runs and errands, cutting day-to-day fuel use.
Covers many NZ urban return trips without using much petrol if you charge at home or work.
When you use the plug-in side properly, total fuel use over a week can be far lower than a standard SUV.

car_specs_benefits.spec: AWD with multiple drive modes (EV, Series Hybrid, Parallel Hybrid)
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Extra traction for wet roads and gravel, with the car able to choose the most efficient mode as conditions change.

cta_mid_page.headline: See if an imported Outlander PHEV stacks up for you
cta_mid_page.subtext: Tell us your budget and how you drive, and we’ll run through real auction examples and landed pricing.
cta_mid_page.button_text: Happy to walk you through the options and pricing - no obligation, just the facts.

models_comparison.intro: Here’s how different 2018-era Outlander PHEV options from Japan compare for NZ buyers.

rows.notes: For Outlander PHEV, 2018-on cars typically meet NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no structural repairs are noted. Electronic stability control is standard by this point, which is important for NZ rules.

Most 2018 Outlander PHEVs use the 2.0L petrol engine with twin motors and a 12 kWh battery. Earlier years can have slightly different tuning and equipment, while later facelifts may add updated safety tech and interior tweaks.

Higher trims from Japan may add leather seats, upgraded audio, adaptive cruise, extra safety systems and nicer interior finishes. Base trims are more modest inside but can still be good buying if you prioritise price over features.

Based on auction documentation, this model usually meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. As a plug-in hybrid, it may attract lower CO2 charges than a comparable petrol-only SUV, but fees depend on final NZ rules at the time of registration.

2018 Outlander PHEVs appear regularly at major Japanese auctions, but clean, low-kilometre, high-grade examples attract stronger bidding. If you’re picky on colour, trim or mileage, allow some time for the right one to come up.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief and give you a cost breakdown before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For plug-in hybrids like the Outlander PHEV, we pay close attention to battery health notes, hybrid system comments and service history on each shortlisted car.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet and our recommendation. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common trims or colours, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing a plug-in hybrid is straightforward, but it helps to be clear on the main risks and how we handle them.

items.risk: Worried about battery degradation and how much EV range is left?
items.solution: We look for recent battery health information, check for EV system warning lights on photos, and favour cars with regular hybrid servicing noted. You’ll see the auction sheet and our comments before you decide to bid.

items.risk: Concerned about hidden costs once the car lands in NZ?
items.solution: We outline a full landed estimate that includes auction price, shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification and typical on-road costs. If something is only an estimate, we say so, so the numbers are clear before you commit.

items.risk: Not sure how to read auction grades or hybrid-related comments?
items.solution: We translate the auction sheet, explain the grade, and highlight any notes about the hybrid system, charging port, brakes or electronics. That way you can see why a particular Outlander PHEV looks like a tidy option or why we’d skip it.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: You tell us the budget, year range and features you care about, plus how and where you drive. We then scan the Japanese auctions, shortlist suitable Outlander PHEVs, and send you real examples with photos, grades and landed pricing. If the numbers and condition stack up for you, we handle the bidding, shipping and NZ entry steps from there.

faq_block.q: What real-world fuel use can I expect from a 2018 Outlander PHEV in NZ?
faq_block.a: Most owners see around 4–5L/100km if they plug in regularly and do a mix of city and open-road driving. If you rarely charge or mostly do long highway trips, fuel use will be higher and closer to a conventional SUV.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import a 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV from Japan?
faq_block.a: Most 2018 Outlander PHEVs we source land between about $22,000 and $35,000, depending on mileage, grade and features. Your total cost includes the auction price, our fee, shipping and insurance, NZ entry certification, and on-road costs like registration and plates.

faq_block.q: Will a 2018 Outlander PHEV meet NZ compliance standards?
faq_block.a: Based on auction documentation, this model usually meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final approval depends on physical inspection in NZ, so we avoid cars with obvious red flags in the paperwork.

faq_block.q: How long does it take from winning an auction to driving the car in NZ?
faq_block.a: From a successful bid, shipping typically takes 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule, then you need time for NZ entry certification and registration. In most cases you’re looking at roughly 6–10 weeks all up, but port congestion and workshop queues can move that around.

faq_block.q: What auction grade should I look for on an Outlander PHEV?
faq_block.a: Grades 4 and above with a tidy interior grade are usually where the better cars sit, but the comments and photos matter as much as the number. We go through the sheet with you, point out any notes on the hybrid system, body repairs or corrosion, and explain why a car looks reasonably well bought or not.

footer_cta.button_text: Happy to walk you through the options and pricing - no obligation, just the facts.

subscription_block.headline: Stay in the loop
subscription_block.subtext: Get occasional updates on Outlander PHEV auction listings, landed price examples and import tips for NZ buyers.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is an NZ-based importer that’s been sourcing vehicles from Japan since 2009. We focus on late-model Japanese cars, hybrids and plug-in hybrids, and give buyers direct access to auction stock. Our role is to make the process transparent so you can decide if importing stacks up against buying locally.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Your landed cost is the total to get a 2018 Outlander PHEV from a Japanese auction to ready for registration in NZ.

rows.notes: The hammer price in Japan plus local auction and agent fees. This varies with mileage, grade and trim level.
Roll-on roll-off shipping from Japan to NZ and marine insurance to cover the vehicle while it’s in transit.
Includes port charges, biosecurity cleaning if required, NZ entry certification, and any work needed to meet NZ standards, plus registration and plates.

auction_checklist.check: Battery SOH or any hybrid system comments on the auction sheet
auction_checklist.why: Gives an early indication of remaining EV range and whether the high-voltage system has known issues.

auction_checklist.check: Warning lights visible in interior photos
auction_checklist.why: EV, hybrid, ABS or AWD warning lights can point to more complex and costly repairs.

auction_checklist.check: Underbody and corrosion notes, especially for snowy regions
auction_checklist.why: Corrosion around suspension or high-voltage cabling can affect both safety and long-term reliability.

auction_checklist.check: Charging port condition and any mention of charging faults
auction_checklist.why: Damaged ports or slow-charging issues can be expensive to sort and reduce day-to-day usability.

auction_checklist.check: Interior electronics and climate control comments
auction_checklist.why: Infotainment and climate systems are common complaint areas and can be fiddly to repair if they’re already playing up.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: You don’t have easy access to home or work charging and rarely plug in, which means you won’t see the main fuel savings.
You prefer a simple, non-electrified SUV and don’t want to think about batteries, charging or hybrid servicing.
You need seven seats all the time — the 2018 Outlander PHEV is typically a five-seat layout.
You mainly do very long open-road trips where a conventional hybrid or efficient diesel might make more sense.

alternatives_block.intro: If the Outlander PHEV doesn’t quite line up with how you drive, these alternatives can be worth a look.

rows.better_if: You want a simpler hybrid system with strong reliability and lower maintenance expectations, and you’re not worried about plugging in.
rows.tradeoff: No plug-in capability or EV-only driving, so day-to-day fuel savings depend more on how and where you drive.

rows.better_if: You prefer a smaller, more urban-friendly plug-in hybrid with similar EV range for city use.
rows.tradeoff: Less cargo space and no AWD option, so it’s not as versatile for rough roads or heavy loads.

rows.better_if: You want a sedan or hatch with strong efficiency and lower running costs for commuting.
rows.tradeoff: Not an SUV, so it’s less suited to gravel roads, higher seating position preferences or larger family gear.

battery_health_block.headline: Checking Outlander PHEV battery health from Japan
battery_health_block.body: On a 2018 Outlander PHEV, the 12 kWh battery’s condition has a direct impact on how much EV-only driving you’ll get and how low your running costs can be. While we can’t test the battery ourselves until the car is in NZ, we use the available Japanese documentation to reduce guesswork before bidding.
checklist: Look for recent battery SOH readings or dealer inspection notes where available.
Avoid auction sheets that mention hybrid system faults, repeated warning lights or no-start conditions.
Prefer cars with regular servicing at Mitsubishi dealers, especially if hybrid system checks are noted.
Consider mileage and usage pattern — high kilometres with little mention of charging may mean more wear on the battery.
Allow some budget for future battery or hybrid system maintenance as the car ages.

charging_block.headline: Charging a 2018 Outlander PHEV in NZ
charging_block.home_charging: Most owners charge at home using a standard NZ power outlet or a dedicated wall unit. Overnight charging is usually enough to refill the 12 kWh battery, so your first 20–35 km of each day can often be electric if you plug in regularly.
charging_block.public_charging: The Outlander PHEV uses a CHAdeMO fast charging port, which is supported at many public DC chargers around NZ, though CCS is becoming more common. You can still rely on AC charging at home and use CHAdeMO when it’s convenient rather than as your only option.
caveats: Public charging costs and availability vary by provider and region, so it’s worth checking local networks you’ll actually use.
CHAdeMO is well established but newer sites may prioritise CCS, so long-term you may lean more on home charging.
Fast charging too often can add extra heat cycles to the battery; regular home charging is usually gentler for day-to-day use.

ev_range_plus_petrol.headline: EV range for the commute, petrol for the long trips
ev_range_plus_petrol.body: The 2018 Outlander PHEV offers EV-only range up to 35 km from its 12 kWh battery, which covers many NZ commutes, school runs and errands if you charge at home. Once the battery is low, the 2.0L petrol engine and hybrid system take over, so you can head out of town without worrying about where the next charger is. Around town it runs mostly on electric, open road the petrol engine takes over seamlessly, which is where the 4–5L/100km real-world figures usually come from.
items: Use EV mode for short city trips to cut fuel use and keep things quiet.
Let the hybrid system manage hills and passing lanes on longer drives.
Plan to plug in most days if you want to see the full running-cost benefit.
For mixed driving, expect fuel use well below a similar-sized petrol SUV in most cases.

phev_use_cases.headline: How a 2018 Outlander PHEV works in everyday NZ driving
phev_use_cases.body: The Outlander PHEV suits a mix of city commuting, family duties and weekend trips, especially if you can plug in at home. Different NZ regions play to different strengths of the plug-in hybrid system.
items: Auckland: Stop–start traffic and city hills suit EV mode and regenerative braking, trimming fuel use and brake wear.
Christchurch: Flat terrain and steady speeds help the hybrid system settle into an efficient rhythm for suburban and highway use.
Wellington: Steep climbs and descents make good use of regenerative braking, though wind and elevation changes can nibble at EV range.
Hamilton: Flat expressways and predictable commutes let the car repeat the same efficient pattern day after day.
Tauranga: Coastal traffic and holiday congestion are where EV mode helps keep fuel use down, while corrosion protection is handy near the sea.

model_deep_dive.headline: 2018 Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV: what you’re actually getting
model_deep_dive.body: This generation of Outlander PHEV pairs a 2.0L petrol engine with twin electric motors and a 12 kWh battery, driving all four wheels. It’s a five-door, five-seat SUV with practical cargo space and a cabin that’s easy to live with for families. Regenerative braking and multiple drive modes (EV, Series Hybrid, Parallel Hybrid) let the car adapt to city, highway and hilly routes without you needing to fiddle too much. From Japan, you’ll see a spread of trims, from simpler cloth-interior models through to higher-grade versions with extra comfort and safety features.
items: 0–100 km/h in around 10 seconds, which is adequate for NZ motorways and passing lanes.
AWD system for extra traction on wet roads, gravel and light off-bitumen use.
Regenerative braking that helps slow the car while topping up the battery on descents.
Five-seat layout with enough space for kids, adults and everyday gear.
Hybrid system that can prioritise EV running in town and blended power on the open road.

trim_comparison.headline: Choosing the right Outlander PHEV trim from Japan
trim_comparison.body: Japanese-market 2018 Outlander PHEVs come in several trims, often with different interior finishes and safety features. Higher grades can be worth the extra auction price if you value comfort and driver-assistance tech, but even mid-level trims can be fair buying if you mainly care about the plug-in hybrid system.
items: Entry and mid trims: Cloth seats, simpler audio, fewer driver-assist features, but the same core plug-in hybrid drivetrain.
Higher trims: Leather or part-leather interiors, upgraded audio, more advanced safety systems and sometimes extra convenience features.
Special packages: Some cars add options like sunroofs or cosmetic upgrades; we’ll flag these when they appear on auction sheets.
Budget vs features: We can show you landed cost examples for different trims so you can decide where the numbers start to make sense.
